mnemonics.title: A.D.C.S. S.T.A.C.K.
mnemonics.content: Aggregate, Dynamic, Counter, Stack operations - All Diligent CS Students Take Advanced Knowledge. This covers the core techniques in amortized analysis.

mnemonics.title: Sam Pushes Apples
mnemonics.content: SAM = Stack Amortized Method. P = Push, A = Array doubling. Helps remember: Push operation cost is O(1) amortized in dynamic arrays.

mnemonics.title: Potential Powers Push People
mnemonics.content: PPP = Potential Function helps Push/Pop operations. The potential energy model tracks stored work for future expensive operations.

mnemonics.title: Accounting = Bank Account
mnemonics.content: Think of amortized cost as 'depositing' cheap operations to 'pay for' expensive ones later. The accounting method is like a savings account for CPU time.

mnemonics.title: Three Methods, One Goal
mnemonics.content: AAA = Aggregate (total cost/n), Accounting (credits), Analysis (potential energy). All three lead to O(1) amortized for dynamic table doubling.

acronyms.expansion: Average Method Of Reducing Time by Offsetting Repeated Expensive Data
acronyms.usage: When you need to explain why expensive operations don't happen frequently enough to affect overall complexity

acronyms.expansion: Property Of Time and Energy that Links Initial and Final States
acronyms.usage: In the potential method, define Φ(Di) where Φ(D0) = 0 and Φ(Di) ≥ 0 for all i

acronyms.expansion: Doubling Yields Efficient Memory Allocation Technique
acronyms.usage: When table load factor α = n/T > 1/2, double size; when α < 1/4, halve size. Ensures O(1) amortized per operation.

acronyms.expansion: Bits Increment Reversally; Carries Yields Counter Overflow Regulation
acronyms.usage: For incrementing a binary counter, each bit flips roughly n/2^i times, giving O(2) = O(1) amortized

visualTips.visualization: Picture a conveyor belt. Individual items may be heavy (expensive), but over time the average weight per item is light. Draw a timeline showing total work done divided by n operations.
Imagine a coin bank. Each cheap operation 'deposits' a coin. Expensive operations 'withdraw' coins. Keep a running balance. Never let balance go negative.

Think of a bouncy ball. When you push down (cheap operation), you store potential energy (compress the ball). When it springs back up (expensive operation), it releases that stored energy. The potential function Φ measures 'spring compression'.

Picture a rubber band expanding. When it gets half-full (50% load factor), snap! It doubles in size. When it gets too empty (25% load factor), it shrinks. The rubber band absorbs the copying cost over many operations.

Watch a odometer in a car. The rightmost digit flips every time. The next digit flips every 2 times, then 4, then 8. Flips decrease exponentially as you move left. Sum = 2n - 1 = O(n) total for n increments.

analogies.analogy: Movie subscription vs pay-per-view. Watching one movie at the theater (worst-case) costs ₹500, but a monthly subscription (amortized) averages ₹50/movie. You might watch 10 movies one month and none the next, but your average stays affordable.
A rechargeable battery. Cheap operations charge the battery (increase potential). Expensive operations discharge it (use stored energy). The battery level (Φ) must always stay above zero.

A growing city's water reservoir. When it's half full, you build a bigger reservoir (double capacity). The cost of building is 'amortized' over all the water you've stored. You don't rebuild for every cup of water.

Cleaning a messy room. Each item you pick up and discard costs energy. But you only clean periodically (when room gets too full). The 'multipop' is like hiring a cleaning service - expensive occasionally, but averages out to minimal daily effort.

A bus seating capacity. If it's too empty (below 25%), you switch to a smaller bus (halve). If it's too crowded (above 50%), you get a bigger bus (double). This keeps average passengers per trip optimal.
